ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Pastries and cakes) used as the closest defensible category for this filled flaky pastry; no tau sar piah-specific code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tau sar piah or equivalent filled Asian pastry; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tau sar piah piece weighs approximately 55 g, which is a typical single-serve portion as sold by traditional Singaporean bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Tau Sar Piah (Sweet Bean) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Tau Sar Piah (Sweet Bean), cr_id F11110028. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 10.5 g/100g based on the known fat profile of lard/shortening-based flaky pastry shells consistent with HPB methodology; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: Biscuits, cookies and crackers — GI analogous estimate based on sweet filled pastry / biscuit category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tau sar piah (sweet bean) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous category of sweet filled shortcrust/flaky pastry biscuits with refined wheat flour and sugar-based filling, consistent with GI values of 60–70 reported for similar products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (55 g × 0.53 = 29.15 g) / 100 = 18.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (22.8 g/100g) with very low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, raising blood pressure risk independently of the low sodium content (29 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium, the high sugar and low fibre profile of tau sar piah (sweet bean) is clinically disqualifying for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit consumption to occasional single-piece servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 22.8 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling, and total carbohydrate at 53 g/100g with only 0.9 g/100g dietary fibre produces very rapid postprandial glucose absorption; estimated GI of 65 and GL of 18.9 per standard serving confirm high glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid tau sar piah (sweet bean) or limit intake to a single small piece on rare occasions, pairing with protein and fibre-rich foods to partially attenuate the glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 10.5 g/100g and trans fat of approximately 0.2 g/100g from lard or hydrogenated shortening, combined with high sugar (22.8 g/100g) and total fat (25.85 g/100g), present a composite atherogenic and pro-inflammatory risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid tau sar piah (sweet bean) due to its high saturated fat, trans fat, and sugar content, all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) and saturated fat (~10.5 g/100g) from shortening, combined with high sugar (22.8 g/100g), drive pro-inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid tau sar piah (sweet bean) due to its pro-inflammatory fat and sugar profile; versions made with lard or hydrogenated shortening are particularly unsuitable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tau Sar Piah (Sweet Bean) is a traditional Teochew Chinese flaky pastry widely sold in Singapore, consisting of a multi-layered, crumbly wheat-flour shell made with lard or vegetable shortening, filled with sweetened mung bean (tau sar) paste. The pastry is baked until golden and has a distinctively flaky, melt-in-the-mouth texture with a sweet, earthy mung bean filling. It is a popular snack and gift item, especially during festive occasions such as Chinese New Year and Mid-Autumn Festival. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 471 kcal, 6.6 g protein, 25.85 g fat (estimated 10.5 g saturated, ~0.2 g trans), 53 g carbohydrate, 22.8 g sugar, 0.9 g dietary fibre, and 29 mg sodium. The high sugar content (22.8 g/100g) and very low fibre (0.9 g/100g) make this the most glycaemically challenging of the tau sar piah variants, with an estimated GI of 65 (medium) and a glycaemic load of approximately 18.9 per standard 55 g piece. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar, saturated fat, and trans fat content. It is vegetarian but not vegan (may contain lard) and is not halal-certified in traditional preparations. The food is also known as dou sha bing in Mandarin Chinese.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat tau sar piah?
How many calories in tau sar piah?
What is the GI of tau sar piah?
Is tau sar piah suitable for hypertension?
What is the difference between sweet and salty tau sar piah?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95 for an exact named entry (F11110028). Saturated fat and trans fat are adapted estimates consistent with the known fat profile of lard/shortening-based flaky pastry and are clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for similar sweet filled pastries.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid breakdown, and moisture data for tau sar piah (sweet bean) are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is set to 0.2 g/100g as an adapted estimate based on the known trans fat contribution of hydrogenated vegetable shortening commonly used in traditional tau sar piah production; this is not directly reported by HPB and is documented as an approximation. saturated_fat_g is set to 10.5 g/100g as an adapted estimate consistent with the fat profile of lard/shortening-based flaky pastry shells. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database sweet filled pastry/biscuit category; no direct measurement for tau sar piah exists. Halal status is uncertain for traditional preparations using lard; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific producer. The is_vegan flag is set to false as traditional preparations use lard; vegetarian status applies only to versions using vegetable shortening.
